article_title: Who will maintain the web when PHP's veterans retire?
core_thesis: The PHP developer workforce is aging, with over half of surveyed developers having more than 15 years of experience and only 15% having five or fewer years, creating a skills gap that threatens the maintenance of the vast amount of PHP-powered web infrastructure. AI-generated code may exacerbate the problem by accumulating silent technical debt, while the lack of junior developers entering the ecosystem makes hiring a top challenge.
detailed_summary: The article, based on the Perforce 2026 PHP Landscape Report surveying over 700 developers, highlights a critical skills shortage in the PHP ecosystem. Over 50% of PHP developers have more than 15 years of experience, while only 15% have five years or less, indicating a maturing workforce with insufficient new talent. Hiring has become the top concern for managers and directors, and 24% of respondents cite lack of skilled personnel as a leading operational challenge. PHP remains foundational, tied with JavaScript as the most used language (72% of participants), powering major platforms like WordPress, WooCommerce, and Wikipedia, and is used primarily for services/APIs (80%), internal business applications (70%), and content management (56%). However, most PHP development now involves small tweaks to existing code rather than new applications, and enterprises tend to avoid PHP for new large projects—82% of PHP-using companies have fewer than 500 employees. The article also notes that AI-generated code may worsen the problem by creating unmaintainable technical debt, and that the skills gap is part of a broader open-source and IT talent shortage, with a recent survey showing acute skills shortages doubling to 10.4% in six months.

entities.name: Brad Shimmin
entities.role: Analyst at Futurum Group, commented on skills gap and AI-generated code risks

entities.name: Matthew Weier O'Phinney
entities.role: Principal product manager at Perforce Zend and OpenLogic, highlighted the open-source nature of the problem

entities.name: Andrew Cornwall
entities.role: Analyst at Forrester Research, noted PHP's ease of learning and its role in existing web infrastructure

entities.name: Adam Culp
entities.role: Manager of professional services at Perforce Zend, wrote about the widening expertise gap

key_data_points: Over 50% of PHP developers have more than 15 years of experience, while only 15% have five years or less, indicating a severe shortage of new talent entering the ecosystem (🟢 relevant to your career planning: as a student, you could fill this gap by learning PHP, making you a valuable asset for maintaining legacy web systems).

Hiring is the top challenge for PHP team managers and directors, and 24% of respondents cite lack of skilled personnel as a leading operational challenge (🟢 relevant to your job search: PHP skills are in high demand but short supply, giving you leverage).

PHP ties with JavaScript as the most used language in 2026 (72% of participants), followed by Python (49%), Node.js (46%), and Java (35%) (🟢 relevant to your programming: PHP remains a dominant web language, so learning it broadens your stack).

82% of PHP-using companies have fewer than 500 employees, meaning PHP is prevalent in small-to-medium businesses rather than large enterprises (🟢 relevant to your career: you may find opportunities in startups or SMBs).
The most used PHP frameworks are Symfony (47%) and Laravel (40%), with Laminas/Mezzio at 8% and CakePHP at 5% (🟢 relevant to your development: focus on Symfony and Laravel for practical PHP skills).

Relational databases are integrated with 92% of PHP applications, followed by Web APIs (83%), filesystem (70%), and key-value storage (60%) (🟢 relevant to your backend development: PHP is tightly coupled with SQL databases).

AI-generated code may worsen the skills gap by creating silent technical debt, as fewer junior developers are available to maintain it (🟢 relevant to your Red Teaming: AI-generated PHP code could introduce vulnerabilities; you can specialize in auditing and securing such code).
conclusions: PHP's aging developer base poses a real risk to the maintenance of a large portion of the web, but this also creates an opportunity for new developers to enter a high-demand niche with less competition.

AI-generated code is not a panacea; it may increase technical debt and security risks, especially in PHP where experienced maintainers are scarce. This opens a role for security-focused developers to audit and harden AI-generated PHP code.

PHP remains a practical language for web development, especially for small-to-medium businesses and content management systems. Learning PHP (particularly Symfony and Laravel) can be a strategic career move for a student looking to differentiate themselves.
personal_relevance_summary: As a programmer and cybersecurity enthusiast, this article highlights a critical skills gap in PHP that you could exploit for career advantage. Learning PHP—especially Symfony and Laravel—positions you to maintain and secure the vast amount of existing PHP code, which is a high-demand, low-competition niche. For Red Teaming, AI-generated PHP code is likely to contain vulnerabilities, making PHP security auditing a valuable specialization. Additionally, the article's discussion of technical debt from AI-generated code aligns with your interest in offensive security: you could focus on identifying and exploiting weaknesses in AI-produced PHP applications. As a student, this is a timely opportunity to acquire a skill that many are ignoring.
extra_advice: Consider building a homelab with a PHP-based application (e.g., a WordPress site with custom plugins) and practice security assessments on it. Also, explore static analysis tools for PHP (like Phan or Psalm) to automate vulnerability detection. This hands-on experience will make you stand out when applying for roles that involve maintaining or securing legacy PHP systems.
